title: Between Fire and Law
subtitle: The Iran, Israel, United States Conflict of 2023, 2026: A Multi-Perspectival Legal, Strategic, and Normative Analysis

sections.label: Abstract
sections.title: Introduction: The Persistence of Law in Armed Conflict

blocks.text: Every war produces its lawyers. The 2023-2026 Middle East conflict was no exception. Within weeks of the Hamas attack on 7 October 2023, the region's battlefields were joined by a second front: the terrain of legal justification. Israel invoked Article 51 to defend its Gaza campaign. Iran invoked the same provision to defend its missile strikes on Israel. The United States offered three distinct legal theories for three distinct military campaigns. Russia cited its Constitution. China cited its neutrality. Each State found in international law exactly what it needed: permission. The pattern would have been familiar to the late-nineteenth-century international lawyers who built this system. They designed it to be flexible. They did not design it to be honest.

The human cost was staggering. OCHA estimates over 46,000 Palestinians killed in Gaza during the 15-month campaign; over 4,000 Lebanese civilians in the Israel-Hezbollah conflict; hundreds more in direct strikes between Iran and Israel. The United States conducted over 170 strikes against Houthi targets in Yemen while maintaining operations in Syria and Iraq. By February 2026, when the United States and Israel launched joint strikes on Iran itself, the conflict had become the most sustained interstate confrontation in the Persian Gulf since the Iran-Iraq War of 1980-1988.

What made this conflict unusual was not the violence but the legalism. Every major military operation came wrapped in a legal brief. Israel cited Article 51 for its Gaza campaign and again for its strikes on Iranian nuclear facilities, this time as "anticipatory self-defence against an existential threat." Iran cited Article 51 for its April 2024 missile attacks on Israel, calling them a response to Israeli strikes on Iranian diplomatic premises in Damascus. The United States offered three legal theories for three campaigns: collective self-defence of Israel, individual self-defence of U.S. personnel, and enforcement of navigational rights under UNCLOS. Each theory was plausible in isolation. None survived contact with the facts.

This Article asks a question that international lawyers prefer to avoid: _when States invoke law to justify their wars, does the law constrain them or merely decorate them?_ The question is not whether the 2023-2026 conduct was legal. That question, as this Article shows, can be answered any way you like, depending on which legal framework you select and how generously you interpret it. The question, rather, is whether the law means anything at all when every belligerent can find a legal theory to fit its preferred outcome.

Three problems structure the analysis. First, the legal standards themselves are indeterminate. The ICJ held in _Nicaragua_ that Article 51 self-defence applies only in response to "armed attacks" attributable to States (Merits, Judgment, I.C.J. Reports 1986, p. 14, paras. 115, 237-238), but left the threshold for non-State actor attacks uncertain (_Wall_, Advisory Opinion, I.C.J. Reports 2004, p. 136, paras. 139-140). The temporal scope of self-defence, whether it extends to "anticipatory" action against future threats, remains contested: the United States claims a broad pre-emptive right; most States and the ICJ do not. The gap between these positions is not a failure of interpretation. It is the structure of the system.

Second, attribution in proxy relationships defeats every available legal test. The ICJ's "effective control" standard from _Nicaragua_ (para. 115) requires State direction over specific military operations, a threshold so high that even obvious State involvement fails to satisfy it. The ICTY's "overall control" test from _Tadic_ (Appeals Chamber, 15 July 1999, para. 131) lowers the bar, but neither standard captures what actually happens between Iran and Hezbollah, or between the United States and Israel. The law of attribution was built for a world of formal hierarchies. It breaks down in a world of informal dependence.

Third, domestic constitutional law has become a mirror of international law's indeterminacy. The U.S. President launched strikes on Iran without Congressional authorization, arguing simultaneously that the operations fell within his Commander-in-Chief authority and that they were too limited to trigger the War Powers Resolution's 60-day clock. The argument cannot work both ways: either the strikes were significant enough to require authorization, or they were too minor to count as war. The Administration claimed both. Iran's Supreme Leader directed military operations without reference to the Majlis, raising the question of whether the Islamic Republic's constitutional separation of powers has any operational significance at all. In both cases, domestic law proved as malleable as the international law it was supposed to enforce.

This Article adopts a methodology of **internal coherence assessment**: measuring each State's conduct against its own declared principles, constitutional procedures, and strategic interests, rather than imposing external normative frameworks. The approach has three virtues. It avoids the charge, routinely levelled against Western-centric international law, that standards are applied selectively. It exposes contradictions that are more damaging to State legitimacy than violations of rules nobody agreed to. And it produces reform recommendations that are institutionally grounded rather than aspirational. A State that violates its own constitutional requirements for going to war has failed on terms it set for itself. No external tribunal is needed. The failure is self-evident.

The Article proceeds through fourteen analytical sections. Sections I-IV provide framework, methodology, chronology, and damage assessment. Sections V-VII present detailed legal analyses of the three primary belligerents: the United States, Iran, and Israel. Sections VIII-IX examine secondary actors whose participation was legally consequential despite not being primary belligerents: Lebanon and the Gulf Arab States. Section X analyzes the maritime dimensions of the conflict, where legal frameworks from UNCLOS and the San Remo Manual intersected with strategic imperatives. Section XI examines the roles of Russia and China as "Great Power" actors whose legal positions shaped but did not determine conflict outcomes. Section XII synthesizes the fourteen perspectives into a unified assessment. Section XIII offers specific institutional reform recommendations grounded in each State's legal architecture. Section XIV concludes.

The central finding is that the most significant legal failures occurred not through violation of external norms but through _internal incoherence_: States acting in contradiction to their own declared values and institutional processes. Executive concentration of war powers without legislative oversight, proxy relationships bypassing formal constitutional authorization, and unresolved tensions between security-alliance commitments and diplomatic-neutrality claims produced the widest divergence between conduct and self-declared standards.
